text.title: Role split — plan strong, execute mid, classify small
text.summary: The three-tier cognitive split, why it dominates the cost-per-feature metric, and how to spot mis-assignments.

section.title: The three roles

list.item: Plan / review — strongest model. Decomposes the task, audits the result, decides whether to redo. ~5% of tokens, ~70% of the quality.
Execute / generate — mid model. Writes the code, drafts the document, runs the tool calls. ~80% of tokens.
Classify / extract / format — smallest model. Picks file paths, labels, normalizes formats, fills schemas. ~15% of tokens.

rule.statement: Pin the strongest model to planning and final review; pin the mid model to execution; pin the smallest model to classification, slot-filling, and reformatting.
rule.rationale: Anthropic's "advisor strategy" and Claude Code subagent guides report ~3x cost reduction at unchanged quality on long-running coding tasks; the small token share of planning means the strong-model premium is paid on a small slice while preserving its leverage.

example.code: # .claude/agents/feature-impl.yaml planner: model: opus role: "decompose feature, list acceptance criteria, write per-step plan" implementer: model: sonnet role: "edit files, run tests, iterate on errors" classifier: model: haiku role: "given plan + repo tree, return list of files each step touches" reviewer: model: opus role: "approve or request changes per acceptance criterion"
example.note: Roles are wired to models, not steps. The plan/review boundary is where the strong model earns its premium.
Pays the strong-model premium on every formatting and classification step. Typical 3-5x cost overrun with no quality gain.

section.reference: Claude Code advisor strategy — Opus/Sonnet/Haiku role split.
Anthropic — Choosing the right Claude model.

section.title: Detecting mis-assignment
section.p: Two diagnostics catch wrong-role assignments faster than human review.

rule.statement: For each role, run a 30-prompt eval against the next-cheaper model and the next-stronger model; promote down a tier if quality holds within 1 pp, promote up a tier if quality falls more than 3 pp.
rule.rationale: The Pareto sweet spot drifts as model versions ship; locking a role to a model on launch leaves cost on the table within months. Periodic ablation keeps the assignment honest.

antipattern.description: Splitting by step rather than role: "step-1 = haiku, step-2 = sonnet, step-3 = opus".
antipattern.reason: Steps with the same cognitive role get different models, breaking the leverage. A long execution step does not need Opus just because it is the last step.

section.reference: Reference Claude Code subagent definitions for the three roles.

see-also.link: How to keep context coherent across model boundaries.
